title: Mujadara
subtitle: Lentils and rice with caramelised onions
excerpt: One of the most satisfying combinations in the Levantine kitchen: brown lentils and long-grain rice cooked together in the same pot, topped with a large quantity of onions fried slowly in oil until they are deep brown, jammy, and slightly crisp at the edges. The onions are the whole point.
sourceNotes: Mujadara is one of the oldest dishes in the Arab culinary tradition. Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) documents its wide distribution across Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, Egypt, and the broader Arab world. The name derives from the Arabic word for 'pockmarked', a reference to the lentils visible through the rice. Similar lentil-and-rice preparations appear in medieval Arab cookbooks.

content.text: The onions require patience. They need to be cooked in a generous amount of oil over medium heat for 30 to 40 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they are a uniform deep mahogany brown. This is not a quick soften or a brief caramelise, the onions should be collapsing, very soft, and deeply coloured, with some crisp edges. Rushed onions produce a pale, sweet result that is completely different from the slow-cooked version.

The lentils are cooked separately until just tender, then combined with raw rice and finished together. The rice absorbs the lentil cooking water and some of the onion oil, which is where much of the flavour comes from. Serve with plain yoghurt and a simple green salad.

Put the {{lentils-brown}} in a saucepan and cover with 700 ml cold water. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a steady simmer and cook for 15 to 20 minutes until just tender. Do not drain. You want the lentils and their cooking water together.

Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook, stirring occasionally, for 30 to 40 minutes until very deeply browned and jammy, with some crisp edges. Season with a little salt as they cook. When done, remove half the onions and set aside for topping. Leave the rest in the pan.

Add the lentils and their cooking liquid to the pan with the onions. Stir in the {{cumin-ground}}, {{allspice}}, and salt. Bring to a simmer and add the {{long-grain-rice}}. Stir once, cover tightly, and cook over the lowest possible heat for 15 to 18 minutes until the rice is cooked and the liquid is absorbed. Remove from the heat and leave covered for 5 minutes. Serve topped with the reserved caramelised onions.

items.fix: Mujadara onions need 30 to 40 minutes at medium heat. High heat browns the outside before the inside is soft; the result is bitter and uneven
items.cause: Cooked on too high a heat or for too short a time
items.symptom: Onions are pale brown rather than deep mahogany

items.fix: The lentil water should be about 300 ml when you add the rice. If you have more, boil it down for a few minutes first. Check the rice at 15 minutes and remove from heat if cooked
items.cause: Too much liquid from the lentils or too long a simmer

items.fix: Salt should be added while the onions cook and again after the rice. The deep flavour of the dish depends entirely on properly caramelised onions
items.cause: Undersalted or onions not cooked long enough

items.fix: Cook the lentils only to just tender. They will continue cooking when combined with the rice. Stop them when they still have a little firmness
items.symptom: Lentils are mushy before the rice is done

content.text: Mujadara is sometimes called 'the dish of the poor' in the Levant. That just means it is the dish everyone eats. It is filling, cheap, and good. The pairing of lentils and rice goes back as far as grain farming in the region. Slow-fried onions on top are what made the dish loved, not merely useful. In Lebanese homes it comes with raw onion, plain yoghurt, and a squeeze of lemon.
